Lake Ice Phenology: Part 1

Author

Hailey Patel

Data

The data for this module are provided within the GitHub repository in the data sub-directory.

  • Data: data/lake_ice_off_data.xlsx

Import and Prep

The data are in a multi-sheet Excel file named data/lake_ice_off_data.xlsx. You will need to use the read_excel() function in the readxl package to import the data into R as a data frame. You can learn more about importing from Excel here.
For some reason, read_excel() does not like relative paths, so we will use the here package to locate your project on your computer. First, install the here package. Then, run the here() function to get the path to your project. Using file.path() appends the results of here() to the location of the data within your project/GitHub repository.

library(tidyverse)
── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.1     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(here)
here() starts at /Users/haileypatel/Downloads/FREC 3044/assignment-4-thehailey
file_name <- file.path(here(),"data/lake_ice_off_data.xlsx")

Now use read_excel() to read in file_name. Remember that Excel files can have multiple sheets.

library(readxl)
ice_data <- read_excel(file_name, sheet = 2)

Now, look at a preview of the data using the head() function. The doy in ice_off_doy stands for “day of the year” and is the day within the year where ice off occurred (January 1 = doy 1). did_not_freeze is a column where 1 = did not freeze and 0 = did freeze.

head(ice_data)
# A tibble: 6 × 4
  lake_name  year ice_off_doy did_not_freeze
  <chr>     <dbl> <chr>                <dbl>
1 Baikal     1869 121                      0
2 Baikal     1870 127                      0
3 Baikal     1871 120                      0
4 Baikal     1872 127                      0
5 Baikal     1873 133                      0
6 Baikal     1874 116                      0

There should be four columns and ~2703 rows.

Tidy the data

We will practice key skills in the module using a focal lake. Use the filter function to filter the more extensive data to only contain the data from Lake Sunapee, a lake in New Hampshire. Filter the data ice_data by the variable lake_name to create a data frame called sunapee.

sunapee <- ice_data |>
  filter(lake_name == "Sunapee")

You will be using the sunapee data for the rest of the module until Question 11

Visualize the data

Question 1:

To visualize the data from Lake Sunapee, create an x-y plot with the year (year) on the x-axis and the day of the year (ice_off_doy) on the y-axis. Plot the points (geom_point) and connect the points with lines (geom_line).

Be sure that the title, x-axis, and y-axis labels are clearly labeled.

Answer 1:

class(sunapee$ice_off_doy)
[1] "character"
sunapee$ice_off_doy <- as.numeric(sunapee$ice_off_doy)
ggplot(data = sunapee, aes(x = year, y = ice_off_doy)) + geom_point() + geom_line() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Ice off Day", title = "Lake Sunapee Ice Off Day Over Time From 1869-2025")

How does ice cover change over time at Lake Sunapee?

We want to model how the ice-off date changes over time. Our approach in this module is to use a linear regression that predicts the ice-off day of year as a function of the year. The lm function will estimate the parameters of the DOY = b + m * YEAR linear regression where b is the intercept and m is the slope.

Question 2:

What do you expect the sign (plus, minus, or no change) to be for the slope of the relationship? Why?

Answer 2:

Since global surface temperatures have been increasing historically, the ice off day should be earlier in the year. This means that the slope sign should be negative since as the year increases the ice off day should theoretically decrease.

Use the lm function to predict ice_off_doy as a function (~) of the year for the data.frame called sunapee. You can learn more about the lm function here

Create an object called fit that contains the results of the linear model.

fit <- lm(ice_off_doy ~ year, data = sunapee)

Then, use the function summary to explore the result of the lm function.

summary(fit)

Call:
lm(formula = ice_off_doy ~ year, data = sunapee)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-28.207  -4.990   1.071   6.463  17.128 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 318.94856   32.61480   9.779  < 2e-16 ***
year         -0.10602    0.01675  -6.331  2.5e-09 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 9.51 on 155 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.2055,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.2003 
F-statistic: 40.08 on 1 and 155 DF,  p-value: 2.504e-09

The results of your linear model are in an object named fit, so you can find the regression coefficients at fit$coefficients[1] (the intercept) and fit$coefficients[2] (the slope). Confirm that fit$coefficients[1] and fit$coefficients[2] match the values expected from the summary command you ran above.

fit$coefficients[1]
(Intercept) 
   318.9486 
fit$coefficients[2]
      year 
-0.1060227

Question 3:

What is the direction of the slope? How does this match your expectations from Question 2?

Answer 3:

The direction of the slope is negative. This matches my expectations from question 2.

Is our model any good?

R2

First, it is important to understand how much variation is explained by the model using the R2 value in the summary. A value of R2 close to 1 means that the predictor (year) explains nearly all of the variation in the variable we are predicting: ice_off_doy. A value of R2 close to 0 explains very little of the variation in ice_off_doy. Since we want to be able to explain how ice_off_doy is changing over time using year, we are aiming for a high R2. A low R2 would indicate that our model isn’t particularly good at explaining what future values of ice_off_doy will be.

Question 4:

What is the R2 for the regression? Based on the R2, is year and ice_off_doy strongly related?

Answer 4: The R2 for the regression is 20%. Based solely on the R2, year and ice_off_day are not strongly related since the value is closer to 0. The proportion of variability in ice_off_day is only 20% explained by the year.

Residuals normally distributed

Second, it is important that the residuals from our linear model are normally distributed. The following is a normal distribution that you can visually compare to:

ggplot(data = tibble(value = rnorm(1000, 0, 1))) +
  geom_histogram(aes(x = value), bins = 20)

We will use a histogram to visually assess whether this requirement is met. First, you will need to calculate the residuals. The residuals are the difference between the ice-off DOY that your linear regression predicts and the observed DOY.

  • Residuals that do not average to zero indicate that your linear regression is biased.

  • Residuals that are widely spread out indicate that your linear regression is not very useful for prediction.

You will use a histogram to examine the residuals. First, the code uses the coefficients to predict the yearly ice-off DOY. Second, calculate the residuals by subtracting the predicted ice-off DOY from the observed DOY.

sunapee <- sunapee %>% 
  mutate(predicted = fit$coefficients[1] + year * fit$coefficients[2], 
         residual = ice_off_doy - predicted) #Observed - Predicted

Question 5:

Use the add_predictions() and add_residuals() functions from the modelr package (installs with the tidyverse but you need to load it with library()) to add the predictions and residuals. This accomplishes the same thing above but doesn’t require you to specifically write our your model with the coefficients (thus is more scalable to other models)

Answer 5:

library(modelr)
sunapee <-add_predictions(sunapee, fit)
sunapee <- add_residuals(sunapee, fit)
sunapee
# A tibble: 157 × 8
   lake_name  year ice_off_doy did_not_freeze predicted residual  pred  resid
   <chr>     <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>
 1 Sunapee    1869         130              0      121.     9.21  121.   9.21
 2 Sunapee    1870         130              0      121.     9.31  121.   9.31
 3 Sunapee    1871         102              0      121.   -18.6   121. -18.6 
 4 Sunapee    1872         124              0      120.     3.53  120.   3.53
 5 Sunapee    1873         126              0      120.     5.63  120.   5.63
 6 Sunapee    1874         129              0      120.     8.74  120.   8.74
 7 Sunapee    1875         132              0      120.    11.8   120.  11.8 
 8 Sunapee    1876         132              0      120.    12.0   120.  12.0 
 9 Sunapee    1877         115              0      120.    -4.94  120.  -4.94
10 Sunapee    1878         108              0      120.   -11.8   120. -11.8 
# ℹ 147 more rows

Now, to visualize the residuals, use geom_histogram() to plot them as a histogram. I recommend using a bin width of 10 in the histogram.

Question 6:

Show Histogram and answer the following question: do the residuals meet the assumptions of the linear regression? (are the residuals normally distributed with the distribution centered around zero)?

Answer 6:

ggplot(data = sunapee) +
  geom_histogram(aes(x = residual), bins = 10)

The histogram seems to be left-skewed which violates the residuals assumption of mean centered around 0. In a normal distribution there should be zero skewness.

The slope is different from zero

A slope could appear to be higher or lower than zero, but the uncertainty in the slope is large enough that there is a chance that the slope value is 0 (thus limiting our capacity to believe the value for the slope is not zero). Here, we will focus on the p-value of the slope. The p-value is the probability of obtaining a slope at least as extreme as the one calculated from the data, assuming the truth of the null hypothesis that the slope is 0 (from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P-value). In this case, what is the probability of observing our slope value assuming the actual slope is 0? Smaller probabilities mean there is a lower chance that our slope is equal to 0.

Question 7:

Based on the summary() above, what is the p-value for the slope?

Answer 7: The p value is 0.0000000025.

The slope physically meaningful

A slope value could have a low probability of being 0 (thus we have a strong belief that it is not zero), but the magnitude of change is functionally meaningless in the physical world (i.e., the rate of change is so small that we would not think much of it). This can occur particularly when analyzing large datasets.

Question 8:

Based on the slope value, how many more years are needed to pass before the lake has a 1 week change in ice off doy? Do you think this is meaningful? Why?

Answer 8: Around 65.91/66 years are needed to pass before the lake has a 1 week decrease in ice off day. This is not meaningful since that number is relatively large for only a week difference.

Overall model assessment

Question 9:

How comfortable are you proceeding with using this linear model for additional analysis and prediction? Why? (I am not looking for your gut answer, rather I want you to apply concepts taught in this module to justify your answer)

Answer 9: Since the model was fitted only on lake Sunapee data using it to predict ice off days for other lakes would yield inaccurate results. However, for further analysis on Lake Sunapee since the p values for the slope are very low this could be used to predict ice off days for upcoming years. Since the R2 value is relatively low I would not be confident on predicted ice off day due to the model missing 80% of the potential variation.

Question 10: Now plot the data for Lake Sunapee (just like above - year on x-axis vs. ice of doy on y axis) but add the line from your regression results (e.g., fit intercept and slope). You will use the geom_abline.

Answer 10:

ggplot(data = sunapee, aes(x = year, y = ice_off_doy)) + geom_line() + geom_point() + 
  geom_abline(intercept = 318.94856, slope = -0.10602) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Ice off Day", title = "Lake Sunapee Ice Off Day Over Time From 1869-2025")

Question 11:

The goal of this question is to make a forecast of the future ice-off day (with uncertainty estimate) using your model.

  • Use the regression model to predict the ice-off day of the year in 2040 (provide answer). Remember that year is in the linear model (y = b + mx).
  • Calculate the 90% confidence interval by using thequantile function to determine the 5% and 95% quantile for your residuals in Question 6 (the 5% should be a negative number and the 95% should be a positive number because your residuals are centered around 0). You will use the vector of residuals from Question 6 in your calculation.
  • Add lower confidence interval (a negative number) and the upper confidence interval (a positive number) to your prediction for 2040 to calculate the 90% confidence interval around your prediction (provide your answer).
  • How wide is your confidence interval (number of days)?

Answer 11:

predicted <- predict(fit, newdata = data.frame(year = 2040))
predicted
       1 
102.6622 
quantiles <- quantile(sunapee$resid, probs = c(0.05, 0.95))
quantiles
       5%       95% 
-19.42732  14.15137 
lower <- predicted[1] + quantiles[1]
lower
       1 
83.23486 
upper <- predicted[1] + quantiles[2]
upper
       1 
116.8135 
width <- lower - upper
width
        1 
-33.57868

The predicted ice off day for year 2040 is 102.6622 or day 103. The confidence interval is (83.23486, 116.8135) and it is around 33 days wide.

Is the ice-off day of the year changing more quickly in more recent years at Lake Sunapee?

To answer this question, you need to divide the Sunapee dataset into two datasets that represent an early and later period. For this analysis, use the year 1970 as a cut-off (one dataset is before 1970, and the other is 1970 and after).

Question 12:

Use the skills developed above to evaluate the question “Is the ice-off day of year changing more quickly in more recent years at Lake Sunapee?”

Answer 12:

Use a mix of code, figures, and text to present your analysis. If you want to compare slopes, be sure they are on the same plot.

early <- sunapee |>
  filter(year < 1970)
later <- sunapee |>
  filter(year >= 1970)

sunapee <- sunapee |>
  mutate(early = ifelse(year < 1970, 0, 1))
sunapee
# A tibble: 157 × 9
   lake_name  year ice_off_doy did_not_freeze predicted residual  pred  resid
   <chr>     <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>
 1 Sunapee    1869         130              0      121.     9.21  121.   9.21
 2 Sunapee    1870         130              0      121.     9.31  121.   9.31
 3 Sunapee    1871         102              0      121.   -18.6   121. -18.6 
 4 Sunapee    1872         124              0      120.     3.53  120.   3.53
 5 Sunapee    1873         126              0      120.     5.63  120.   5.63
 6 Sunapee    1874         129              0      120.     8.74  120.   8.74
 7 Sunapee    1875         132              0      120.    11.8   120.  11.8 
 8 Sunapee    1876         132              0      120.    12.0   120.  12.0 
 9 Sunapee    1877         115              0      120.    -4.94  120.  -4.94
10 Sunapee    1878         108              0      120.   -11.8   120. -11.8 
# ℹ 147 more rows
# ℹ 1 more variable: early <dbl>
fit_early <- lm(ice_off_doy ~ year, data = early)
summary(fit_early)

Call:
lm(formula = ice_off_doy ~ year, data = early)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-26.870  -5.005   1.498   6.446  16.511 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 325.54205   62.51505   5.207 1.04e-06 ***
year         -0.10967    0.03257  -3.367  0.00108 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 9.544 on 99 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.1027,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.09367 
F-statistic: 11.34 on 1 and 99 DF,  p-value: 0.001084
confint(fit_early, level =0.9)
                    5 %         95 %
(Intercept) 221.7426103 429.34148988
year         -0.1637522  -0.05558389
fit_later <- lm(ice_off_doy ~ year, data = later)
summary(fit_later)

Call:
lm(formula = ice_off_doy ~ year, data = later)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-25.2986  -5.2371   0.7928   6.2643  17.3062 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 711.92157  148.89946   4.781 1.38e-05 ***
year         -0.30239    0.07454  -4.057 0.000161 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 9.016 on 54 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.2336,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.2194 
F-statistic: 16.46 on 1 and 54 DF,  p-value: 0.0001613
confint(fit_later, level = 0.9)
                    5 %       95 %
(Intercept) 462.7286556 961.114475
year         -0.4271407  -0.177644
ggplot(data = sunapee, aes(x = year, y = ice_off_doy, color = as.factor(early))) + geom_point() + 
  geom_segment(aes(x = 1869, y = 120.56882, xend = 1969, yend = 109.60182), color = "red") +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 1970, y = 116.21327, xend = 2025, yend = 99.58182), color = "blue") + 
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Ice off Day", title = "Slope for Early Period (Before 1970) and Later Period (After 1970)")
Warning in geom_segment(aes(x = 1869, y = 120.56882, xend = 1969, yend = 109.60182), : All aesthetics have length 1, but the data has 157 rows.
ℹ Please consider using `annotate()` or provide this layer with data containing
  a single row.
Warning in geom_segment(aes(x = 1970, y = 116.21327, xend = 2025, yend = 99.58182), : All aesthetics have length 1, but the data has 157 rows.
ℹ Please consider using `annotate()` or provide this layer with data containing
  a single row.

For the early period, the slope was -0.10967 with a p value of 0.00108 showing that we can reject the null hypothesis. However, this slope is fairly flat meaning that prediction in the earlier period would be less meaningful. Since the slope is flatter, it would take a higher number of years for a meaningful decrease in the ice off day. In addition, the R2 is also 0.1027 which means that 90% of the variation is not explained by the regression line. For the later period, the slope was -0.30239 with a p value of 0.000161 showing that we can reject the null hypothesis. This slope is relatively steeper than the one seen for the early period. This means that in this time range the ice off day changes more quickly throughout the years. The R2 is also higher with 23.36 of the variation in the Y explained by the regression. I also computed the confidence interval for the slope for both fits and found that the intervals do not overlap showing that we are 90% confident that the slopes are different for the two fits. This analysis of slopes can also be seen in the graph that after 1970 the slope seems to get steeper.

Rendering and committing

Remember to Render your document as HTML and comment+push to GitHub your code and rendered HTML that was created when you rendered the document. Your GitHub repository should have multiple commits with informative commit messages.

Proper documentation of AI-generated assistance includes the prompt, the source (e.g., ChatGPT), and the significant parts of the response. Proper documentation of discussion with classmates include listing their names and the components discussed.

Model: Gemini3 Prompt: can you control where the abline starts in r ggplot2

Answer: No, you cannot directly control where geom_abline() starts and ends within ggplot2, as it draws a line that extends to the limits of the plot panel. To draw a line that has specific start and end points, you should use the geom_segment() function instead

Using geom_segment()

The geom_segment() function allows you to specify the exact start and end coordinates of a line segment.

x: starting x position

y: starting y position

xend: ending x position

yend: ending y position
